content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN. You can perform the following operation on it:


Choose any index iii such that 1<i<N1 \lt i \lt N1<i<N and AiA_iAi​ is not smaller than either of its neighbors, i.e, both Ai≥Ai−1A_i \geq A_{i-1}Ai​≥Ai−1​ and Ai≥Ai+1A_i \geq A_{i+1}Ai​≥Ai+1​ are true.

Then, decrease AiA_iAi​ by 111.
Is it possible to make all the elements of AAA equal by performing this operation several (possibly, zero) times?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains NNN — the number of elements in AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, denoting the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `"Yes"` if it is possible to make all the elements of AAA equal, and `"No"` otherwise (without quotes).


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `NO`, `no`, `No`, and `nO` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible to make all elements equal using the allowed operations, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: single element, already equal. Test case 2: edges are 1 and all elements are >=1, so YES. Test case 3: first element 2 != last element 3, so NO.

solution.approach: The operation can only decrease a element that is not smaller than both of its neighbours. The first and last elements can never be chosen, therefore they never change. Consequently, in any successful sequence all elements must become equal to the original value of A1 (which must also equal AN). Hence a necessary condition is A1 == AN. Moreover, because we can only decrease values, every interior element must be at least A1; otherwise it could never be increased to match the edges. These two conditions are also sufficient: while there exists an interior index i with Ai > A1 and Ai >= Ai‑1 and Ai >= Ai+1, we can repeatedly decrease Ai. Since the edges stay at A1, this process eventually makes every element equal to A1. Thus the answer is "YES" iff A1 == AN and for every i, Ai >= A1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case – a single pass to check the two conditions.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
        ok = arr[0] == arr[-1] and all(v >= arr[0] for v in arr)
        out.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
